chunk_summary.idea_trajectory_justification: The group continues to brainstorm and explore various approaches, including tandem catalysis, supercritical fluids, and dual-functional materials, without converging on a single project.
chunk_summary.collective_engagement_justification: Non-speaking participants consistently maintain eye contact with the screen and provide occasional visible head nods, indicating active listening.

chunk_summary.problem_specificity_justification: Participants discuss specific challenges like C-C coupling and dilute streams, but have not formulated a shared research question.
chunk_summary.decision_crystallization_justification: The group is still generating and sharing ideas from their respective fields without selecting one to pursue together.
chunk_summary.funding_reference_description: Gary Moore mentioned pitching a Scialog proposal on a related topic last year that was not funded.
chunk_summary.notable_observation: Gary Moore shared that he pitched a similar idea at the previous Scialog that was not funded, which prompted sympathetic laughter and highlighted the difficulty of the problem.

ideas_currently_on_table: Dual-functional materials for DAC and thermocatalysis
chunk_summaries: The session began with round-robin introductions followed by a structured minute of silent writing to generate discussion questions. Michael sought clarification on whether converting CO2 to fuels counted as negative emissions, which Emily clarified as zero emissions. The group then transitioned into divergent ideation, with Michael proposing a focus on the dynamic speciation and solvation of active sites in electrocatalysis, noting Emily's prior work in this area. The tone was professional and structured, ending with the group beginning to explore specific mechanistic challenges.

The group engaged in divergent exploration of challenges in electrocatalysis, focusing on the difficulty of probing reactive intermediates and catalyst degradation. Participants shared their methodological expertise, with Emily highlighting how Zhou's quantum modeling could fill gaps left by experimental limitations. Rob introduced a shared Google document for notes and shifted the conversation toward defining the ultimate goal, questioning whether they should aim for fuels or solid carbon products. The tone remained collaborative and engaged, though no specific project was decided upon.

Emily Carter facilitated a structured round-robin, calling on each participant to share their individual research interests and ideas. Participants proposed various angles on electrocatalysis, including the electric double layer, active sites, computational descriptors, and the continuum from molecules to materials. The discussion remained highly divergent as members shared their domain knowledge without converging on a specific joint project. The tone was professional and attentive, with participants actively listening and occasionally nodding.

The group continued divergent exploration of electrocatalysis challenges, with Zhou Lin discussing the limitations of computational models and the potential of machine learning. Ellen Matson introduced the role of charge compensation and Lewis acids, bridging molecular and materials science perspectives. Emily Carter managed the meeting time and synthesized contributions, while Gary Moore and Kelsey Hatzell highlighted the difficulties of breaking scaling relationships and bridging model systems with real-world performance metrics. The tone remained collaborative and intellectually engaged, though the group has not yet converged on a specific joint project.

The group continued divergent ideation, exploring tandem catalysis, carbamate reduction, and the use of supercritical CO2 to alter reaction mechanisms. Leslie and Ashleigh bridged thermocatalysis concepts with electrocatalysis, proposing dual-functional materials for direct air capture and subsequent reaction. Gary shared a past failed proposal on a similar topic, prompting sympathetic laughter. The tone remained highly collaborative and engaged, with participants actively building on each other's ideas, though they have not yet converged on a specific joint project.
